metaTitle: Enigma Explained — Rotor Cipher History and Simulation
metaDescription: Learn what the Enigma machine was, how rotor encryption worked, and why it matters historically even though it is not modern security.

quick.what: Simulates the German Enigma rotor cipher machine.
quick.use: Learning how mechanical encryption worked before modern digital cryptography.

intro.title: In plain English
intro.text: Enigma was an electro-mechanical cipher machine that scrambled letters using rotating components. It was famous during World War II and is now one of the best teaching tools in cryptography history.

how.title: How it works
how.text: When you press a key, the signal passes through a plugboard, rotating rotors, and a reflector, then returns along a different path to light up the encrypted letter. Because the rotors move after each keypress, the substitution keeps changing.

useCases.title: Where you'd use it
items: Understanding how changing substitution differs from simple ciphers like Caesar.
Teaching the historical link between cryptography, war, and computing.
Experimenting with rotor settings to see how tiny changes affect output.

mistake.title: Common mistake
mistake.text: It is easy to assume Enigma was unbreakable because it looked incredibly complex. In practice, operator habits, captured materials, and deep analysis made it vulnerable.

history.title: History / fun fact
history.text: Enigma is historically important not just because it encrypted messages, but because the effort to break it helped push forward modern cryptanalysis and early computing.

security.title: Security note
security.text: Enigma is a historical system and should be treated as an educational simulation, not as a serious protection method.

sections.title: Why rotor stepping mattered
sections.text: The rotor positions changed after each keypress, so the same letter could encrypt to different outputs as the message progressed.

sections.title: Why it was eventually broken
sections.text: Enigma's strength depended on both machine design and operator discipline. Repeated message habits, known phrases, and mathematical analysis all helped cryptanalysts reduce the search space.

diagram.title: Signal path through a rotor machine
diagram.caption: The moving rotors are what make Enigma more interesting than a fixed alphabet substitution.
